entry_points.description: Login endpoint accepting username and password as query string parameters, performs direct SQL query against users table

auth.notes: No real authentication mechanism. Passwords stored and compared in plain text in SQLite. No session or token issuance on successful login — response is simply a success string. No logout or session management.

data_flows.name: User login flow
data_flows.validation: None. No input validation or sanitization is performed on username or password.
data_flows.processing: Values are directly interpolated into a raw SQL string template literal and executed via db.get()
data_flows.storage: In-memory SQLite database, users table with columns: id, username, password
data_flows.output: Plain text HTTP response: 'Login successful!', 'Login failed', or 'Error'
data_flows.sanitization_notes: No parameterized queries, no ORM, no escaping. User input is directly concatenated into the SQL query string via template literal interpolation at app.js:16.

trust_boundaries.description: HTTP client to Express application on port 3000
trust_boundaries.enforcement: No CORS configuration, no helmet security headers, no rate limiting, no HTTPS enforcement. Express defaults only.

trust_boundaries.description: Express application to in-memory SQLite database
trust_boundaries.enforcement: No parameterized queries. Raw string interpolation used to construct SQL. No ORM layer or query builder.

modules.name: application
modules.description: Monolithic single-file application containing all server setup, database initialization, route handling, and authentication logic

security_observations: Credentials (username and password) are passed via GET query string parameters, which will be logged in browser history, server logs, and proxy logs
User-supplied input is directly interpolated into SQL query strings using ES6 template literals with no parameterization or escaping (app.js:16)
Passwords are stored in plain text in the database with no hashing (app.js:9)
No input validation or sanitization is performed on any user input
No security headers middleware (e.g., helmet) is configured
No rate limiting is applied to the login endpoint
Error messages are generic but the raw SQL query is logged to console on every request (app.js:18), which would expose injected payloads in server logs
The application returns no session token or cookie on successful authentication — there is no session management
The SQLite database runs in-memory with no persistence, seeded with a single hardcoded admin credential
No HTTPS or TLS configuration is present
